issue_title: TracePlugin not working for bottle + python 2.7.x
issue_body:
Hi, we have a backend using python 2.7.x, Im integrating Datadog APM following the documentation and I have the following error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bottle.py", line 764, in _handle
    return route.call(**args)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bottle.py", line 1575, in wrapper
    rv = callback(*a, **ka)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ddtrace/contrib/bottle/trace.py", line 32, in wrapped
    resource = "%s %s" % (request.method, request.route.rule)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bottle.py", line 1237, in __getattr__
    raise AttributeError('Attribute %r not defined.' % name)
```

when I go to trace.py line 32, I find this:
`resource = "%s %s" % (request.method, request.route.rule)`

somehow request.route doesn't exist, I think that probably is related to a different python or bottle version being used on our end.
Anyway `route` is already provided as a parameter for this method (`TracePlugin.apply(self, callback, route):`) so there's no need to call `request.route.rule`, you can call `route.rule` instead,
`resource = "%s %s" % (request.method, route.rule)`

Otherwise let me know if you have more details about this error and what's the right way to solve it,
thanks

issue_title: unnecessary exception thrown when quitting gecko driver
issue_body:
Using gecko driver 0.18 on an ubuntu installation with firefox 53.0 and when running the simple splinter test example provided in the docs, the following exception is thrown. 

`E       selenium.common.exceptions.WebDriverException: Message: quit`

It seems that when the quit function is called on the driver, gecko driver is throwing an exception instead of exiting gracefully. 

Please see this stack overflow link for a similar description of the issue

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45505441/webdriverexception-message-quit

With Webob 1.3.1 and Pylons 1.0.1, I get the following:
```
Error - <type 'exceptions.AttributeError'>: 'NilAccept' object has no attribute '_parsed'" while reading upstream
```
The code is relying on an internal attribute which isn't present for all Accept types.  It looks like ab588e7e59a2f44f6b3678db6d735870a385333c introduced this and reverting it makes things work again.

issue_title: Ability to specify client hostname for connection
issue_body: We have application that needs to pass hostname of it's real client (instead of the API server's hostname) in client_hostname of LOGINREC structure. Thus it would be helpful for cTDS to support client_hostname argument in cTDS.connect() (in the same way as currently existing appname)

issue_title: repo.index create new index on ever reference - surprising
issue_body:
was surprised to find that the index property of Repo created a new IndexFile object on each reference. This would have lead to code with terrible performance if I had not read the source and discovered this. At a minimum this needs a warning in docs.

I would suggest that you either make index stable until the user asked for it to be updated.
Or drop the property in favor of a function that returns a new IndexFile().

```
import git

```

I think that the GitPython commit is causing a corruption of this file:
    .git/logs/refs/heads/pr-cmd-raise-with-stderr-on-error

I think it is supposed to be 1 line per commit with the first line of the commit at the end.
But I have all of the log message in the file that GitPython attempts to parse and errors out on.

Goals:

* Top-level objects (request, response, headers, transport) should look and feel the same for both client and server use-cases.
* Design for the most common use-case, req/res, then add in streaming as an alternative and more intensive api. Don't have streaming pieces lying around by default, you should have to add those in manually.
* Create consistency between schemes, such that future schemes would look similar to existing and users would intuitively know how to use them.
* Response should be the same object on both client and server if possible.
* Match other TChannel implementations where possible, without offending Python idioms.

issue_title: Date arguments raise ctypes.ArgumentError when a datetime value is used
issue_body:
I'm trying to call a COM method defined as follows (copy-pasted from the API docs, as I don't have access to the IDL):

    Function ExportCalibrationToDCM(
        ExportFileName As String,
        [ExportChangedOnly As Boolean = False],
        [ExportFullDataItemNames As Boolean = False],
        [UseDisplayFormat As Boolean = False],
        [ExportDCM2Format As Boolean = False],
        [ExportComments As Boolean = False],
        [ExportFunctions As Boolean = False],
        [ExportUnits As Boolean = False],
        [FilterFileName As String],
        [ModificationSource As String],
        [ModificationFromDateTime As Date],
        [ModificationToDateTime As Date],
        [FilterMatchOption As VISION_EXPORT_FILTER_MATCH_OPTION = VISION_EXPORT_FILTER_MATCH_AND_AXIS_ITEMS]
    ) As VISION_STATUS_CODES

The code that comtypes generates for this method looks like this:

    COMMETHOD([dispid(1610743868), helpstring('Exports a DCM calibration file (*.dcm)')], HRESULT, 'ExportCalibrationToDCM',
              ( ['in'], BSTR, 'ExportFileName' ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], VARIANT_BOOL, 'ExportChangedOnly', False ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], VARIANT_BOOL, 'ExportFullDataItemNames', False ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], VARIANT_BOOL, 'UseDisplayFormat', False ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], VARIANT_BOOL, 'ExportDCM2Format', False ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], VARIANT_BOOL, 'ExportComments', False ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], VARIANT_BOOL, 'ExportFunctions', False ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], VARIANT_BOOL, 'ExportUnits', False ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], BSTR, 'FilterFileName', '' ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], BSTR, 'ModificationSource', '' ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], c_double, 'ModificationFromDateTime', datetime.datetime(1899, 12, 30, 0, 0) ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], c_double, 'ModificationToDateTime', datetime.datetime(1899, 12, 30, 0, 0) ),
              ( ['in', 'optional'], VISION_EXPORT_FILTER_MATCH_OPTION, 'FilterMatchOption', 1 ),
              ( ['out', 'retval'], POINTER(VISION_STATUS_CODES), 'Status' )),

When I try to call this with just the first argument, or explicitly call it with a datetime object as `ModificationFromDateTime`, I get the following exception:

    ctypes.ArgumentError: argument 11: <class 'TypeError'>: wrong type

I have to explicitly set the two date arguments to `0` in order to make the call succeed:

    s.ExportCalibrationToDCM('c:\a.dcm', ModificationFromDateTime=0, ModificationToDateTime=0)

issue_title: Environment variables in Pipfile are not being expanded when executing pipenv lock
issue_body:
We want to use a private git repo as a dependency for a project. We added the following line in our Pipfile:
`"private" = {git = "https://gitlab-ci-token:${CI_JOB_TOKEN}@gitlab.private.com/path/to/repo.git", editable = true, ref = "v0.0.4"}`
When running pipenv install everything works fine but when running pipenv lock it fails because the environment variable is not being expanded.

I think this is a side effect of this PR: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/pull/2141

issue_title: "setup.py install" does not coexist nicely with "pip install" for namespace packages
issue_body:
An [exhaustive test](https://github.com/jonparrott/namespace-pkg-tests) of how namespace package installation works across `pip install`, `pip install -e`, `python setup.py install`, and `python setup.py devlop` reveals that the following cases fail:

1. Installing one package in the namespace using `setup.py` and the other using `pip` (order does not matter).
2. Installing one package in the namespace using `setup.py develop` and the other using `setup.py install` (order does not matter).

You can see the entire matrix of scenarios [here](https://github.com/jonparrott/namespace-pkg-tests/blob/master/table.md). Note that pep420 packages are failing on 2.x presumably because of #406.

Context: https://github.com/pypa/python-packaging-user-guide/issues/265#issuecomment-276939459

issue_title: Incorrect body/headers returned when werkzeug.router raises a redirect
issue_body:
As of v10.0, `werkzeug.routing` raises a `RequestRedirect` (subclass of `HTTPException`) when you pass an URL without a trailing slash, trying to redirect it to the corresponding canonical URL with the slash.

However, what appears to me to be a bug in Flask's error handler causes the returned redirect response to have mismatching body and headers:
 - `werkzeug.routing` raises `RequestRedirect` ([wkz/routing.py:1421](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/werkzeug/blob/0.10/werkzeug/routing.py#L1421))
 - `RequestRedirect` is subclass of `HTTPException` but overridden to return a customized `Response`, as created by the `redirect` helper function. ([wkz/utils.py:338](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/werkzeug/blob/0.10/werkzeug/utils.py#L338))
 - The exception bubbles up through Klein's `Deferred`, and eventually ends up in the failure handler `processing_failed`. ([klein/resource.py:220](https://github.com/twisted/klein/blob/release-15.0.0/klein/resource.py#L220))
 - The handler checks for a `HTTPException` and then starts to propagate it up to response. ([klein/resource.py:234](https://github.com/twisted/klein/blob/release-15.0.0/klein/resource.py#L234))
 - However, instead of setting everything using the response object obtained by calling `he.getResponse()`, it takes only the headers from it. The error code and body are taken directly from the exception with `he.get_body()`.
 - The `HTTPException.get_body` method is programmed to return a fixed string depending on the error code represented by the class. It ignores the overridden `getResponse` from `RequestRedirect`. ([wkz/exceptions.py:113](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/werkzeug/blob/0.10/werkzeug/exceptions.py#L113))
 - The headers set in [klein/resource.py:238](https://github.com/twisted/klein/blob/release-15.0.0/klein/resource.py#L238) and the body returned in [klein/resource.py:241](https://github.com/twisted/klein/blob/release-15.0.0/klein/resource.py#L241) mismatch.

Notably, the headers of the `RequestRedirect` response include `Content-Length` of the original message. With the request actually returning a different body, with a different actual length, this causes problems. For example, curl will hang forever, trying to read all the bytes which the header says the response has, which will never arrive.

issue_title: Mapping key syntax error parsed as if there is no issue

```js
var jsYaml = require("js-yaml")

const str = `
myObj:
  a: "something"
  b: "some other thing" c: "third thing"
`

jsYaml.safeLoad(str)
```

### Expected result

An error, maybe something along the lines of `bad indentation of a mapping entry at line 4, column 24`?

### Actual result

```js
{  
  myObj:{  
    a:'something',
    b:'some other thing',
    c:'third thing'
  }
}
```

issue_title: scikit-image sprint at BIDS May 28 - Jun 1
issue_body:
## Description
This issue collects ideas and issues for the scikit-image side of the joint scikit-learn/scikit-image/dask sprint at UC Berkeley. 

- work on the parallelization of some algorithms to improve performance, focussing maybe first on the case of machines with several cores (rather than a distributed architecture, which most users don't use I guess). This would need some benchmarking and we could test several solutions such as dask (with Matt Rocklin, we could test and maybe improve our apply_parallel) as well as joblib and its different backends (with Gaël and Olivier). Emma can provide some tomography datasets for this, it would be better if we had a machine with 10-20 cores to ssh on for benchmarking, maybe with AWS? Or a BIDS machine?
- discussing all [GitHub "needs decision" PRs/issues](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/labels/status%3A%20needs%20decision). 
- get nD transforms, based on NumPy array coordinates, working. (with @kne42)
- figure out whether we can reliably distribute Numba, and if so, do we want to get that dependency.
- implement flood-fill with support for lowlevelcallables (see https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/2876#issuecomment-385241507)
- fix data types and ranges. (See [#3009](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/3009).)
- discuss and implement possible matmul interface for transforms instead of add. (with @hmaarrfk)
- talk about release schedules, governance, funding, leadership, outreach, and a few more general topics. (Though @jni thinks these things should happen in the evenings over dinner etc. ;)
- deprecate the skimage viewer to move it to its own repo.

Stuff that we want to happen *before* the sprint:
- [ ] announce the sprint on the mailing list to see whether anyone wants to join remotely
- [ ] release 0.14
- [ ] get airspeed velocity working (though maybe this needs to happen *before* the sprint anyway)
- [ ] draw up scikit-image 1.0 roadmap (including emailing mailing list for community discussion)

Note also [this repo](https://github.com/scisprints/2018_05_sklearn_skimage_dask) for ideas for *joint* work with sklearn and dask teams. I suspect @jakirkham will be the ringleader for that. =)

issue_title: Consider not converting images when using `img_as_float` on a float32 image
issue_body: See e.g. #1627

There are a number of options to improve on the current init signature. Right now it's a list of parameters with defaults.

What's wrong with the current situation?
- A bunch of arguments with defaults is a bit brittle. We have to change the signature every time we add new options.
- In https://github.com/airbrake/airbrake-python/pull/53 I added **kwargs for variable arguments at the end. Configuration values are in named arguments with defaults, anything setting contexts ends up in the variable arguments.

The only real alternative I can think of is to pass the context by ref in one arg and create it separately from the `Airbrake` object.

Given the condition for selecting winner columns during the local inhibition step here:

https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/f1dd3ca676891f7370cf5404b3bbe432ac503f75/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1471

I believe there is a bug that allows columns to be activated that have no overlap with the input vector. When the condition is updated to ensure the column overlaps with the input vector, the proper behavior is observed.

Before:

[![](http://img.youtube.com/vi/w1t4quc1oNs/0.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w1t4quc1oNs)

After:

[![](http://img.youtube.com/vi/BiMZvSbGZKs/0.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BiMZvSbGZKs)
Part of #2000

I discovered this while working on the integration of Win64 build of nupic.bindings into Bamboo-CI.

The call to numpy's `import_array` fails and emits "ImportError: numpy.core.multiarray failed to import".

For some reason, we weren't executing py_region_test.exe in our AppVeyor build. However, when I did a test run with py_region_test.exe, it failed the same way in AppVeyor.

I initially narrowed down reproducibility to the following combination of calls before running out of time in the current dev cycle:
```
  nupic::NuPIC::init();
  nupic::PyRegion::NTA_initPython();
```

The failure manifests itself in the scope of `nupic::PyRegion::NTA_initPython()`, and goes away if your skip the call to `nupic::NuPIC::init()`.

In the current ElasticSearch integration we have 1 index called `records` and inside a document type called `record`. In order to be production ready, these are some changes that can be introduced:

* [ ] Allow separating document types in different indexes. This will make our integration [ES 2.0 ready and avoid conflicts in mappings](https://www.elastic.co/blog/great-mapping-refactoring) between different document types.

* [ ] Use aliases on top of indexes for zero downtime (https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/guide/current/index-aliases.html).

* [ ] Allow each overlay to have its own mappings. Currently, the mapping is [hardcoded is `es.py`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/master/invenio/ext/es.py)
    * We can have a registry of mappings as JSON files and map (based on name?) index -> mapping

https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-accounts/blob/next/invenio_accounts/ext.py#L87

or perhaps use pkg_resources to detect if celery is installed.
Run [release checklist](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/wiki/Release-preparation-checklist) step 1-3

issue_title: ES query parser inconsistency comparing to Q on empty string and no parameters
issue_body:
I don't know if this is a bug or a planned design, but if you compare the IQ function from `invenio_query_parser.contrib.elasticsearch` and the standard Q function from `elasticsearch_dsl.query` there is a difference on how they behave when initialized without parameters and with empty string.
```python
from elasticsearch_dsl.query import Q
Q()
# MatchAll()

from invenio_query_parser.contrib.elasticsearch import IQ
IQ()
# TypeError: invenio_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'pattern'
```

and
```python
from elasticsearch_dsl.query import Q
Q('') 
# ** elasticsearch_dsl.exceptions.UnknownDslObject: DSL class `` does not exist in query.

from invenio_query_parser.contrib.elasticsearch import IQ
IQ('')
# MatchAll()
```

would it make sense to make the IQ behave the same way as Q?

issue_title: Spurious test failure on slow machines
issue_body:
Greetings.  I recently packaged zope.testrunner for the Fedora Linux distribution.  Fedora supports 3 primary architectures: x86_64, i686, and ARM.  We run zope.testrunner's own test suite as part of the build process.  The tests all pass on all 3 architectures, but the ARM builders always report a test failure, because this line is expected:

Total: 321 tests, 0 failures, 0 errors and 0 skipped in N.NNN seconds.

but this is what is actually produced:

Total: 321 tests, 0 failures, 0 errors and 0 skipped in 2 minutes N.NNN seconds.

Is there any way to make the "N minutes" phrase optional on that line?

issue_title: Add function for max compressed size
issue_body:
Unless I'm missing something, Brotli doesn't include a function to calculate the maximum size of a compressed buffer given a specified input size (*i.e.*, the brotli version of zlib's `compressBound` function). I've been using uncompressed_size + 5 in squash, but that doesn't work with larger buffers. Based on some testing with blobs of random data, the points where the output size changes are:
Max input size Max compressed extra bytes
64 KiB 4
8 MiB 5
9 MiB 8
16 MiB 9
24 MiB 13
??? 16
24 MiB + 1 bytes requires input_size + 16, but I stopped testing there. For now I'm going to use `uncompressed_size + 5 + ((uncompressed_size / (1024 * 1024 * 8)) * 4)` in Squash, but I would feel better if you provided a function or macro.

issue_title: Automatically associate resources when flag is given
issue_body:
If I want to create a user and add this user to both an organization and a team, I have to use the following commands to accomplish the task in 3 lines.

``` bash
tower-cli user create --username="gdorf" --password="password" --email=asdf@asdf.com
tower-cli organization associate --organization="Hyrule Ventures" --user=gdorf
tower-cli team associate --team=QA --user=gdorf
```

It would be more user-friendly if this were doable in one line. This statement could create the user and then execute the two subsequent associations automatically.

``` bash
tower-cli user create --username="gdorf" --password="password" --email=asdf@asdf.com --organization="Hyrule Ventures" --team=QA
```

The resources that contain associate and disassociate commands are the following, given in a "parent - child" format:

- organization - user
- organization - project
- host - group (note: the order seems backward here)
- team - user

We would want to add fields onto `create` and `modify` commands for the child resources user, project, and group.

### Implementation

This is just my own 2 cents, but looking at `lib/tower_cli/models/fields.py`, it seems logical to add a special field property that marks the field as an `associated_field` (for an example). Then in both the create and modify methods (in `base.py`), all fields marked as `associated_field` would be removed from kwargs, and put into their own list. After the resource was successfully created or modified, _assoc or _disassoc could be called at the end of the method in order to process the special (parent resource) field.

On the other hand, since this only covers a few cases, it might be easier to override create/modify methods for each child resource. This would also allow easier use of the nested endpoint like `parent/{id}/child` and avoid an extra API call. There are several valid approaches.

issue_title: Gradient clipping is not consistent with other frameworks
issue_body:
Right now dynet clips gradients separately for each parameters. This means that if you clip your gradients to 1.0 but have 16 `Parameter` objects, the total gradient will be clipped to a norm of 4.

The correct thing to do would be to clip the total norm, like pytorch does https://pytorch.org/docs/master/_modules/torch/nn/utils/clip_grad.html

I've found that it makes a difference (in LM experiments)

issue_title: When to renew the CG when working with multiple modules?
issue_body:
Hi,

When I use multiple modules within a single model, I notice that the training time increases significantly as the model is exposed to more training examples. I believe that there is something wrong in the way I am renewing the computational graph while combining the different modules but am unable to find it. Will appreciate help in debugging this. I am doing this:  For instance, if I have two different modules A and B such that A calls B, I am renewing the CG before calling A (for every example).  Here is an example module that uses 4 layer FFNNs as encoder and decoder networks in a VED ( Variational Encoder Decoder)

The loss function of the VED is as follows:
```
  def calculate_loss_basic(self, input, output):
       # Renew the computation graph
       #dy.renew_cg()  -> Not renewing the CG here but in the calling script that instantiates this.

       # Instantiate the params
       W_mean = dy.parameter(self.W_mean_p)
       V_mean = dy.parameter(self.V_mean_p)
       b_mean = dy.parameter(self.b_mean_p)
       W_var = dy.parameter(self.W_var_p)
       V_var = dy.parameter(self.V_var_p)
       b_var = dy.parameter(self.b_var_p)
       num_hidden = self.hidden_list[-1]
       
       enc_dnn = FeedForwardNeuralNet(self.model, [self.num_input, self.hidden_list, num_hidden, self.act])
       dec_dnn = FeedForwardNeuralNet(self.model, [num_hidden, self.hidden_list,self.num_output, self.act])

    
       # Get the output from encoder
       src_output = enc_dnn.predict(input)

       # Get the mean and log variance
       mu = self.mlp(src_output , W_mean, V_mean, b_mean)
       log_var = self.mlp(src_output , W_mean, V_mean, b_mean)       
    
       # Compute the KL Divergence loss
       kl_loss = -0.5 * dy.sum_elems( 1 + log_var - dy.pow(mu, dy.inputVector([2])) - dy.exp(log_var))

       # Reparameterize
       z = self.reparameterize(mu, log_var)
       
       return kl_loss, dec_dnn.calculate_loss(z , output)

```

The corresponding function in the FFNN is as follows:
```
  def predict(self, input):
    weight_matrix_array = []
    biases_array = []
    acts = []
    for (W,b, act) in zip(self.weight_matrix_array, self.biases_array, self.act):
         weight_matrix_array.append(dy.parameter(W))
         biases_array.append(dy.parameter(b))
         acts.append(act)
    g = acts[0]
    w = weight_matrix_array[0]
    b = biases_array[0]
    intermediate = g(w*input + b)
    activations = [intermediate]
    for (W,b, act) in zip(weight_matrix_array[1:], biases_array[1:], acts):
        pred =  act(W * activations[-1]  + b)
        activations.append(pred)
    return pred
```

The FFNN itself is declared as follows:

```
  def __init__(self, model, args):
    self.pc = model.add_subcollection()
    self.args = args
    self.num_input = int(args[0])
    self.num_output = int(args[2])
    self.hidden_list = args[1]
    self.act = args[3]
    self.model = model
    self.number_of_layers = len(self.hidden_list)
    num_hidden_1 = self.hidden_list[0]
    
    # Add first layer
    if debug == 1:
        print "Adding input to first hidden layer weights ", num_hidden_1, self.num_input
    self.W1 = self.pc.add_parameters((num_hidden_1, self.num_input))
    self.b1 = self.pc.add_parameters((num_hidden_1))
    
    # Add remaining layers
    self.weight_matrix_array = []
    self.biases_array = []
    self.weight_matrix_array.append(self.W1)
    self.biases_array.append(self.b1)
    for k in range(1, self.number_of_layers):
              if debug == 1: 
                   print "At ", k , " adding weights ", self.hidden_list[k], self.hidden_list[k-1]
              self.weight_matrix_array.append(self.model.add_parameters((self.hidden_list[k], self.hidden_list[k-1])))
              self.biases_array.append(self.model.add_parameters((self.hidden_list[k])))
    if debug == 1:
      print "Adding last hidden layer to output weights ", self.num_output, self.hidden_list[-1]
    self.weight_matrix_array.append(self.model.add_parameters((self.num_output, self.hidden_list[-1])))
    self.biases_array.append(self.model.add_parameters((self.num_output)))
    self.spec = (self.num_input, self.hidden_list, self.num_output, self.act)


```

The calling script is as follows:

```
     for (input_frame, output_frame) in zip(frames_handled_input, frames_handled_output):
       dy.renew_cg()  -> Renewing the CG here
       frame_count += 1
       count += 1
       KL_loss, RECONS_loss = ved.calculate_loss_basic(dy.inputTensor(input_frame), dy.inputTensor(output_frame))
       loss = dy.esum([KL_loss, RECONS_loss])
       train_loss += loss.value()
       kl_loss += KL_loss.value()
       recons_loss += RECONS_loss.value()
       loss.backward()
       if frame_count % update_params == 1:
            trainer.update() 
       if frame_count % 10000 == 1:
               print "Train Loss after epoch " +  str(epoch) + " and frames " + str(frame_count) + " : " +  str(float(train_loss/frame_count)) + " in files ", K , " of " , len(train_data)
               print "Recons_loss: ", float(recons_loss/frame_count)
               print "KL Loss: ", float(kl_loss/frame_count)
               end_time = time.time()
               print "Time: ", end_time - start_time

Hi,

I have to connect to a mail server (Exchange) where emails are used as usernames. At the moment `HttpNtlmAuth` constructor finds `@` in my username [here](https://github.com/requests/requests-ntlm/blob/master/requests_ntlm/requests_ntlm.py#L31) and splits it into a domain and username pair.

This was added in @nitzmahone's pull request https://github.com/requests/requests-ntlm/pull/68.

In this particular case this is incorrect as domain should be empty. But it may also be incorrect in the general case. According to [this](http://davenport.sourceforge.net/ntlm.html#nameVariations), domain should always be empty when username is in kerberos-style format.

It is easy to work around this as I can change domain after `HttpNtlmAuth` was constructed. But should we add optional `domain` parameter to the constructor and/or not split usernames?

Regards,
Srdan

```
λ pip freeze | grep ntlm
ntlm-auth==1.0.2
requests-ntlm==1.0.0
```

issue_title: [BigQuery] Feature Request - An easy way to get a QueryResults from a QueryJob
issue_body:
Problem:

There isn't an easy way to fetch the row results of a `client.run_async_query(job_id, query)` call.

I have to do some yucky stuff to get it to work in a sample I'm working on.

    client = bigquery.Client()
    query_job = client.run_async_query(str(uuid.uuid4()), query)
    query_job.use_legacy_sql = False
    query_job.begin()

    wait_for_job(query_job)

    query_results = bigquery.query.QueryResults('', client)
    query_results._properties['jobReference'] = {
        'jobId': query_job.name,
        'projectId': query_job.project
    }

Possible Solutions:

- Modify constructor for QueryResults or add a class function to take an optional job object.
- Add a method to QueryJob `get_query_results()` that does this same yuckiness (modifying the internal `_properties`)

I'm sure there are other solutions, too, which is why I'm asking for input rather than going off and trying to fix right away.

Reference:

- QueryJob: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/bigquery/job.py#L933
- QueryResults: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/bigquery/query.py#L42
- Docs: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/master/docs/bigquery-usage.rst Notice the async query does not show fetching row results.

issue_body:
The [delete verb for datasets](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/datasets/delete) has a `deleteContents` parameter.

> If True, delete all the tables in the dataset. If False and the dataset contains tables, the request will fail. Default is False

We do not currently expose this parameter in [delete_dataset()](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/reference.html#google.cloud.bigquery.client.Client.delete_dataset).

I could see this parameter being very handy in integration and snippets tests, where we want to delete a dataset and all the tables we have created.

issue_body:
It does [LBYL check][1]

```python
if self._helper_threads.get(name) is None:
    ...
```

but then later does a

```python
self._helper_threads.pop(name, None)
```

after doing some work. So two threads could both pass the first check and then both try to `.pop`.

----

Worth noting:

- Since `.pop(name. None)` is used, if multiple threads call `.pop()`, this won't fail
- The `helper_thread.thread.is_alive()` check could also be problematic since multiple threads could receive `True` for that and then both true to put `STOP` on the queue and `.join()` the thread

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/905f49bd608e8d01d39b718962de6b9c85bb7617/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/_helper_threads.py#L87

issue_title: GoTest does not trigger resolution of .TestImports
issue_body:
This leads to the test binary failing to compile.

Right now GoTest and GoCompile both depend on GoFetch via their GoWorkspace superclass, but they have no way to communicate their dep resolution needs since there is no go_tests target.  Introducing these targets would be one way to solve the problem, fetching both .Imports and .TestImports and classifying these under different products would be another.  A third would be a parameter to ensure_workspace or else a method to override or a constructor parameter to pass to super.

issue_title: `from fs import safe_filename` may not generate unique filenames
issue_body:
The `safe_filename` method can collide if an input shorter than the max length is provided that is itself a valid digest.

One approach to solving this would be to always digest. Another way would be to know the digest length up front, and ensure that any un-digested inputs are strictly shorter than the digest length.

We're making a lot of the datatypes to exist as v2 arguments to rules, and a lot of them have type-checks on their arguments. We should make it easy to write something like:
```
class CatExecutionRequest(typeddatatype('CatExecutionRequest', [('shell_cat_binary', ShellCat), ('input_file_globs', PathGlobs)])
```

to reduce boilerplate.

issue_title: client.logs(stream=True) is broken when using docker 1.7
issue_body:
@shin- 

The streaming functionality of client.logs is broken when using docker 1.7.  I am working on a fix and have found the reason that it is now broken:
Line line 130 in the file daemon/logs.go
```
// write an empty chunk of data (this is to ensure that the
// HTTP Response is sent immediatly, even if the container has
// not yet produced any data)
outStream.Write(nil)
```

This messes up how `_multiplexed_response_stream_helper` works (will break if it sees a response like this).  


The thing I am really wondering though, is why the test to catch this in integration_test.py has been commented out since this commit 8a63e70b in June 2016? This was a commit by you, @shin-.  It seems like this is the exact reason you have integration tests, to catch bugs.   I am not sure of the the best way to proceed given that I cannot show you a failing test.   I would really appreciate it if you could explain:

1. Why the test was removed
2. Steps I need to take to put the tests back and merge my fix. 


Alex

The `Client` class performs a lot URL construction like this:

        res = self._get(self._url("/exec/{0}/json".format(exec_id)))

This can be used to manipulate the query string if `exec_id` contains characters like `'?'` and `'/'`, which can lead to vulnerabilities elsewhere.  The client code should be fixed to reject such invalid parameters.

Downstream bug report: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1248031

I am guessing that one can use default_url_params to globally specify refresh_auth=True on every request?

If so it would be great to document this explicitly.

(Incidentally - are there any published wrappers using refresh_auth that I can take a look at?)

RFC 6455 says:

> The masking key is a 32-bit value chosen at random by the client.
> When preparing a masked frame, the client MUST pick a fresh masking
> key from the set of allowed 32-bit values. The masking key needs to
> be unpredictable; thus, the masking key MUST be derived from a strong
> source of entropy, and the masking key for a given frame MUST NOT
> make it simple for a server/proxy to predict the masking key for a
> subsequent frame. The unpredictability of the masking key is
> essential to prevent authors of malicious applications from selecting
> the bytes that appear on the wire.

But if I'm reading the code right, autobahn appears to [select masks using random.getrandbits](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/57c2f87d7750fa76165a06f2140b7f33f2f093d0/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py#L1800), which uses Python's default Mersenne Twister-based RNG. The Mersenne Twister is inappropriate for security-sensitive applications because its future outputs can be predicted by observing previous outputs. (I.e., seeing the first N masks lets you predict the future masks.)

issue_title: Add instructions for getting AllenNLP to run on Windows
issue_body:
It's currently a pain, apparently, and I'm not sure how much work we should do to support it (if you're reading this and want Windows support, add a comment please).  But @OyvindTafjord was able to get it actually working by modifying some things:

- Got pytorch from 'conda install -c peterjc123 pytorch cuda80' (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/494)
- Pip install of spacy (and other things) required some VS2015 library stuff (had to be same version as used to compile python).
- Pip install of sanic failed (because a dependency, uvloop, is not available for Windows)  [ got around it by remove serve from commands ]
- In general 'pip install -r requirements.txt' worked weirdly and I ended up running each line individually, I didn't dig into why that was.
- Downloading dataset from S3 failed, I think because of non-allowed double quotes in the filename (from the etag) [ got around it by removing etag part of filename]
- Download also threw exception because it couldn't remove the temporary file, seems like Windows hangs onto the file for a bit, a 1 second pause didn't help [ got around by catching that exception, deleting the files manually later ] 
- Had issues getting appropriate tensorflow/tensorboard installed where SummaryWriter was found [ got around it by instead using TensorboardX which somebody made for this purpose ]

I'm going to mark this issue as very low priority, until we hear people actually asking for us to support this.  For now, the instructions will just live in this issue.

issue_title: convert color movies to 8-bit for pims.Video?
issue_body:
I am trying to use trackpy for locating in RGB video images.  At the moment I have written a wrapper to convert the video, using skimage.color.rgb2gray() for each pims image, and then I do my processing.  I am wondering if there is a better way to do this, e.g. a pims way to deal with color video images.  

pims.ImageSequence has the option 'as_gray', but this does not seem to be a choice for other image types.  Alternatively, there is the process_func argument where I could write my conversion, but again this seems to be only for ImageSequence.

Am I missing something obvious?

issue_title: Add http check in addition to the port open check
issue_body:
In https://github.com/deis/deis/pull/3156 we check if the port is open before publishing the container. This works OK for most of the cases but it seems that there's a marginal chance that port is open but the server hasn't finished initializing yet. 

A possible solution would be to add an additional HTTP status check: if port open, send a 
`HEAD / HTTP/1.0` and expect a `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`. 

How does that sound?

$ globus endpoint search
Globus CLI Error: A Transfer API Error Occurred.

request_id:       B1eYyBvVj
code:             BadRequest
message:          scope 'all' requires an additional filter parameter
Low priority, but it'd be convenient if we can do `graphistry.plot(nx.lobster(..)))` without needing any name binding.

Thank you for this package!

Somewhat related to #356 , as far as I understand currently the input format is a pandas.DataFrame, then data is aggregated by the `date` and  `uid` and finally features are extracted from individual time series. All functions in [`tsfresh/feature_extraction/feature_calculators.py`](https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/blob/master/tsfresh/feature_extraction/feature_calculators.py) appear to take as input either 1D arrays or `pd.Series`. 

What I am wondering about is whether computing features from 2D arrays (with axis `('uid', 'time')`) would be computationally more efficient.

I have done a few performance benchmarks to investigate this by comparing,

  *  A. applying tsfresh directly to extract features on a columnar pd.DataFrame 

with aggregating the DataFrame by user and date, to convert it to a labelled array (with xarray, for convenience)

  *  B. followed by computing individual features manually with _do_extraction_on_chunk
  *  C. re-implementing a few metrics in a vectorized fashion and applying it on the xarray directly

The results are available in the following notebooks,
 * [tsfresh_benchmark.ipynb](https://gist.github.com/rth/78c45b1d19b71f6c86abef4824fdf0d7#file-tsfresh_benchmark-ipynb) run on a small [sample_dataset.csv.gz](https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/files/1751897/sample_dataset.csv.gz) (0.2 M rows, 10MB CSV uncompressed)
 * [tsfresh_benchmark_large.ipynb](https://gist.github.com/rth/78c45b1d19b71f6c86abef4824fdf0d7#file-tsfresh_benchmark_large-ipynb) (second notebook in the gist, 14M rows, 700 MB CSV uncompressed), I can provide the data if necessary. 

The conclusion appears to be that feature extraction on 2D arrays would be 10-30x faster. The parallelization is disabled, and these benchmarks are run on a 2 CPU core laptop for the following features `{'abs_energy': None, 'absolute_sum_of_changes': None}`. So even even the default multi-threading in BLAS for numpy operations would not explain this difference. 

In the end I'm trying to run feature extraction from tsfresh on ~5 GB of data (i.e. 10x larger than the larger benchmark) and in my rough estimation this would take half a day for all features. Of course dask parallelization can help, but I'm wondering if there is a computationally more efficient way of doing it before parallelization (that would also work with xarray). 

Am I missing something? What do you think? I imagine in the use case for which this package was developed pandas.DataFrame made more sense than e.g. xarray to store time series data?  Thank you.

issue_title: conda build also define C_INCLUDE_PATH and CPLUS_INCLUDE_PATH
issue_body:
"conda build" currently defines the environment variable INCLUDE_PATH=$PREFIX/include.  However, gcc doesn't use this, but instead uses C_INCLUDE_PATH and CPLUS_INCLUDE_PATH.

Because of this, all of my build.sh files look like this

C_INCLUDE_PATH=$PREFIX/include make install

Instead of the simpler

make install

Should these env vars also be defined?  Thanks

I have been attempting use build features for the packaging of the cling c++ interpreter. Indeed `cling` depends on a patched version of `clang` and `llvm`.

Before I get into build features, the Dependency schema is

```
LLVM [cling]  <------ Clang [cling] <--------- Cling
```

Also, at runtime, Cling depends on `libcxx`, the clang c runtime.

**First approach with features**

Following the approach with the `Debug` empty package in the documentation, I created an empty conda package called `cling-patches` which tracks the `cling`  feature.

```
package:
  name: cling-patches
  version: 1

build:
  number: 0
  track_features:
    - cling
```

Then, I built two variants of Clang, one of which declares a `cling` features and depends on `cling-patches` while the other variant does not. 

My initial understanding was that installing `LLVM`, `Clang` and `cling-patches` together would result in have the cling variants of everything, while it did not.

In fact, the even while depending on `cling-patches`, the default variant of the LLVM package is always chosen! Looking into how others used build features, I came across the statement

https://github.com/conda-forge/blas-feedstock/blob/master/recipe/meta.yaml#L19

```
    # The "blas" package must always track a feature, else it will be
    # prioritised over all others.
```

I ended up having rely on this behavior to create the first dependency chain.

** The new approach **

The current approach is that llvm and clang both track features (`llvmcling` for `llvm` and `clangcling` for `clang`).  Besides, `clang` has `features: llvmcling`. This seemed to work, and to make cling-patches unnecessary.

Now, I am building a recipe (`cling`) that depends on `clang [cling]` and `llvm [cling]`. The build of cling randomly switches between the `cling` and `default` variants of llvm and clang...

Now I am observing he the following behavior: subsequent builds of the same conda recipe results in clang 3.9.1 with both default and cling variants.

issue_title: Curator rollover documentation does not match Elasticsearch rollover API documentation
issue_body:
### Expected Behavior
Curator should have similar controls and behavior as Elasticsearch API

### Actual Behavior
*curator*
When choosing conditions, either one of max_age or max_docs, or both may be used. If both are used, then both conditions must be matched for the rollover to occur.
https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/rollover.html

*elasticsearch*
Elasticsearch also supports `max_size`, and will rollover if *ANY* condition of the three possible conditions is met.

https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-rollover-index.html#CO150-2

In order to shrink an index the shards must first all be routed to a single node. The shrink action will attempt to do this, but if any allocation filters are set in the templates for the index to be shrunk, then the routing allocation will fail. It would be helpful if shrink had an option to clear all route filters before attempting to move shards before the shrink. The option might simply be supplied as a boolean like reset_routing: true

issue_title: Multiple workers: seperate package or included in aiohttp
issue_body:
## Background

gunicorn, tornado, sanic & japronto all use the same system of multiple processes connecting to the same socket to utilise multiple CPUs. Unlike tornado sanic and japronto; aiohttp does not have built-in support for multiple workers, instead relying on gunicorn or a proper reverse proxy like nginx, this must have been a conscious decision but I can't find it documented anywhere.

## Question

If I want this functionality should I submit a PR to include it in aiohttp or create a new package?

## Why not gunicorn?

Gunicorn has been a trusted friend for many years, and I'm using it in production on numerous projects. For projects using uwsgi it's invaluable.

However, it's a victim of its own success/age and much of it's API is far out of sync with python 3.5/6. It's also pretty big and unwieldy as it was designed for wsgi and to do lots more than just connect multiple processes with to the same socket and supervise them.

Also as the docs say:

> But nothing is for free: running aiohttp application under gunicorn is slightly slower.

## The Replacement

Whether the replacement for gunicorn is builtin or a separate package it can be extremely concise and easy to reason with:

```python
from multiprocessing import Process
from socket import SOL_SOCKET, SO_REUSEADDR, socket
from aiohttp import web

def serve_multiple(app, workers):
    sock = socket()
    sock.setsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    sock.bind(('0.0.0.0', 8000))
    sock.set_inheritable(True)

    processes = []
    for i in range(workers):
        process = Process(target=web.run_app, name=f'worker-{i}', kwargs=dict(app=app, sock=sock))
        process.daemon = True
        process.start()
        processes.append(process)

    try:
        for process in processes:
            process.join()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        pass
    finally:
        for process in processes:
            process.terminate()
        sock.close()

async def index(request):
    return web.Response(body='hello world')

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', index)
serve_multiple(app, 4)
```

(this works and gives almost 4 times the request throughput of just `run_app`).

The only thinks which would need to be added for production (AFAIK) are:
* logging
* restarting workers on failure
* request time limits, this could be implemented as aiohttp middleware
* an equivalent of gunicorn's `max_requests` which gracefully kills that worker, then restarts it
* anything else?

issue_title: Jupyter notebook key-binding management is very cumbersome
issue_body:
I'll preface this by saying that jupyter notebooks are awesome and I'm extremely grateful to you guys for creating this tool. I use it all the time for my research.

I often mess with the mappings in my `.vimrc` while writing code with VIM, and change my iTerm2 key bindings. So I'm used to having total, autocratic control over my key-bindings for the tools I use. But so far this seems very difficult to do cleanly with jupyter notebooks.

* When using the help-menu GUI to change mappings, the results appear to be **local to the current *session* of the current notebook**. Re-freshing the notebook (even after saving it) deletes these changes, and the changes are not synced across other existing notebooks.

* I see the actual bindings are stored in `.jupyter/nbconfig/notebook.json`. However if I directly edit this file, there is no way to have my edits reflected in currently existing notebooks. (Also I thought creating new notebooks would reflect any changes to `notebook.json` -- but I recently tried this, and now I'm not sure of the behavior there; jupyter seems to just always load the default mappings, even though the `notebook.json` file has changed).

* Another quirk is that, when using the GUI to change mappings, they are permanently added to the global `notebook.json` file (and new mappings of the same key will overwrite old mappings) -- despite the fact that the mappings are reset to the defaults if the notebook is reloaded!

Let me know if you can't reproduce some of these issues. My jupyter version is 4.2.1.

Would love to see in a future version some more clean controls for jupyter notebook key-bindings -- perhaps a page on the official documentation about changing `~/.jupyter/nbconfig/notebook.json`, and having notebooks automatically scan this file just like they seem to automatically scan the `~/.jupyter/custom/custom.css` and `~/.jupyter/custom/custom.js` files.

issue_title: CollectMineralShardsFeatureUnits scripted agent does not select units fairly.
issue_body:
Thanks for the wonderful framework!

It seemed strange to me that the `CollectMineralShardsFeatureUnits` agent was achieving lower scores (~75) than `CollectMineralShards` (~90) even though it was written to control two units independently rather than as a group and thus could theoretically cover more ground in the same time.

It appears that `CollectMineralShardsFeatureUnits` does not select and issue move commands to units fairly, often resulting in the agent getting stuck alternately selecting units without performing any move action at all, or a single unit making multiple movement actions while the other unit sits idle.

This issue seems to stem from the order of units in `obs.observation.feature_units` being indeterminate across timesteps. In the [current code](https://github.com/deepmind/pysc2/blob/161dfa6ad5db097d42d024afbaf03657e431eb44/pysc2/agents/scripted_agent.py#L99), the index of the current marine within this list is stored and alternated between 0 and 1. The order of the list can change across timesteps, so a stored list index cannot be used to reliably refer to the same unit in a different timestep.

Here's a quick capture of the issue:
![collect_mineral_shards_feature_units](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11659716/37571912-3ab19bcc-2afb-11e8-9638-05622eb5d3de.gif)

issue_title: Removing state should not reschedule all handlers
issue_body:
From the docs: "removing a state causes the queue to be immediately re-evaluated. This ensures that a handler is never invoked when the state is no longer active"

Rather than tossing out the current queue of handlers and re-evaluating it completely, it would be better if the queue was scanned and handlers that should no longer trigger removed.

```python
def add_source(source, key):
    fetch.add_source(source, key)
    reactive.set_state('apt.needs_update')

@reactive.when('apt.needs_update')
def update():
    fetch.apt_update()
    reactive.remove_state('apt.needs_update')
```

In the above example, multiple handlers in the current iteration can call add_source. The slow update handler will not be called until after the current iteration of handlers has been exhausted and a new generation scheduled. Unless one of the handlers calls reactive.remove_state, causing the queue to be immediately reevaluated and this optimization lost.

Or is what I suggest already how it is implemented, or causes some other problem?

issue_title: Feature request: provide way to acquire latest, stable 2.1 Windows/macOS bits
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Starting in .NET Core 2.1, the .deb/.rpm acquisition experience changed. Linux users can now reference `dotnet-sdk-2.1` and `dotnet-runtime-2.1`. This makes it easy to upgrade by just running `apt upgrade`. There is no way to get the equivalent behavior on Windows and macOS

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I'd like a way to be able to get the latest, stable `2.1` runtime and SDK. I'm not concerned about floating the patch version.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
* Update `dotnet-install.ps1` to support a "2.1" stable channel.
* Use a stable link that I can curl/wget from such as `https://aka.ms/dotnet/2.1/dotnet-runtime-2.1-win-x64.{exe,zip}`

**Additional context**
https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-build/pull/1397#issuecomment-393988234

issue_title: Tmux right side only visible for first 3 windows
issue_body: When I first start tmux, with no prior sessions, the right side of the status bar appears as normal (with uptime, time, server name, etc.). However, after I add three additional windows (for a total of four), the Powerline data changes as follows: * If `powerline-daemon` **is** running, then the right side of the status bar changes to the first line of the powerline-daemon usage message: `usage: powerline-daemon [-h] [-r MODULE] [-w WIDTH] [--last-exit-code INT]`. * If `powerline-daemon` **is not** running, then the right side of the status bar becomes blank. If I kill or start powerline-daemon while inside a tmux session, the status bar will adjust to the corresponding appearance after a moment. This is not an issue of *how many* tmux windows I have. If, after creating new windows, I switch back to one of the first three windows, the status bar goes back to normal. Switching back to window 4+, the status bar goes wrong again. This is not an issue of the window number. If I destroy window 1 (where the status bar was correct), and then create another window, that new window will be numbered 1, but the status bar won't be correct for it. I suspect this is because the "pane ID" always increases; although window numbers get re-used, pane IDs do not. The tmux window count applies across sessions, so if I "use up" the correctly working windows in one session, disconnect from that session, and create a new session, *all* the windows in the new session will have the wrong status bar. Killing the tmux server (as by exiting all sessions) and restarting it makes new windows have the right status bar, but only the first three windows again. If I run the tmux command `run-shell 'powerline tmux right'`, the output appears correct. Likewise if I run env "$POWERLINE_COMMAND" $POWERLINE_COMMAND_ARGS tmux right -R pane_id=\`tmux display -p "#D"\`, which is what's configured in *bindings/tmux/powerline-base.conf*. (In my environment, `POWERLINE_COMMAND` is simply `powerline`, and `POWERLINE_COMMAND_ARGS`.) I'm using Powerline 2.2 installed with `pip3 install powerline-status` under Python 3.4 and Tmux 2.0. What else can I do to investigate this problem?

issue_title: why don't spotify player status in MacOS be prefered to use osascript?
issue_body:
in /powerline/segments/common/players.py line 374
```
if 'dbus' in globals() or not sys.platform.startswith('darwin'):
```
this means that even in macOS dbus is preffered way.
but in macOS dbus-python is very buggy, dbus module is buggy no matter installed by brew or ports.
on the contrary, osascript is preinstalled on OS X systems and rarely have issue.

I think that line should be something like
```
if 'dbus' in globals() and not sys.platform.startswith('darwin'):
```

or even just
```
if not sys.platform.startswith('darwin'):
```
because linux system has to use dbus anyways.

issue_title: Would C++ Standard Library and robustness PRs accepted?
issue_body:
Hi!

I'm what one might call a casual code reader. With a quick glimpse, it looks there are issues with 32-bit and 64-bit portability (e.g. see at http://www.viva64.com/en/a/0050/ on using `for(int i ` instead of `for(ptrdiff_t i`, http://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/types/ptrdiff_t) and maybe a possibility to introduce more C++ Standard Library usage. Would such PRs be accepted? Would the test base be exhaustive enough (or should one be created)? Just in case I get time to contribute something by next summer. :)

**<edit:** 
Taking cues from my perhaps too hasty comments from #715, maybe it would be more accurate to ask if there is information on:

1. What quality to target? I'm probably biased from a practical engineering standpoint, but I see strictly standards compliant and paying attention to https://github.com/isocpp/CppCoreGuidelines. Otherwise it is rather difficult for the more casual readers make small quality improvements and consequently both prevent bit-rot and keep difficult-to-reproduce bugs cropping up. This could allow other kinds of improvements which would attract more people.
2. What C++ standard to target? Maybe put otherwise, which platforms or which compilers? This would help to perhaps improve automated testing suites such as in g++, VC++ and Clang.
3. To attract more people with less or no contraint-solver experience, a clear set of guidelines on what is desired and accepted as contributions (you could encourage starting a PR early on to discuss and/or have a Gitter channel) and a buttery smooth setup (maybe something that could be improved too?) would be most helpful.
4. Maybe the juiciest target for a novice to this codebase, would a testing framework be welcomed? It looks like the test suite is [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/tree/master/src/test). It might be possible to add a more rigorous suite and if it were OK, introduce a suite for performance testing too. I'm more familiar with something such as [BenchmarkDotNet (BDN)](https://perfdotnet.github.io/BenchmarkDotNet/Overview.htm) than any C++ tools in this space. Which brings again one question, would it be OK to introduce something like this if it is cross-platform, but on another framework? It could be used in the .NET project already existing. In this particular case, the dependencies are only a few megabytes. // On another note, I see there's https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3test, but the same question applies. Additional one is that if the tests should be easily consumed from the same repo, say if someone works on VC++ and iterates over changes.

Looking at #715 rather superficially, I got the impression the memory management, for instance, might rely on behaviour that is undefined (pointer packing) or introduce alignment errors (not sure, just looking at it).
In addition to grokking the codebase

Maybe it'd be useful to detail the constraints and perhaps a roadmap in Readme.md.

issue_title: z3 python: LShR fails for args with different bit-sizes
issue_body:
LShR fails when both args (BitVecs) have different size, resulting in an ugly exception.

Example code:

```python
import z3

p = z3.BitVec('p', 8)
k = z3.BitVec('k', 16)
z3.LShR(k, p)
```

This results in:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "z3_test.py", line 5, in <module>
    z3.LShR(k, p)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/z3/z3.py", line 3873, in LShR
    return BitVecRef(Z3_mk_bvlshr(a.ctx_ref(), a.as_ast(), b.as_ast()), a.ctx)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/z3/z3core.py", line 1996, in Z3_mk_bvlshr
    raise Z3Exception(lib().Z3_get_error_msg(a0, err))
z3.z3types.Z3Exception: Argument p at position 1 does not match declaration (declare-fun bvlshr ((_ BitVec 16) (_ BitVec 16)) (_ BitVec 16))
```

I think it should be possible to use LShR with BitVectors of different sizes, the shift count bit-size is most of the time much smaller than first argument's bit-size.
If it's not possible to fix it, it would be nice to at least have a nicer exception message that a lisp listing ;)

Hi there,
while rewriting the documentation, I realised that `FS.appendbytes` raises `ValueError`, but shouldn't: according to doc of `ValueError` and `TypeError`, `ValueError` should be raised when given *Inappropriate argument value (of correct type)*, while `TypeError` should be raised when given *Inappropriate argument type*.

Since the exception is raised when `FS.appendbytes(path, data)` is called with `data` not being a `bytes` instance, the exception should definitely be a `TypeError`.

issue_title: at() method hangs when passed negative integers
issue_body:
Running this code results in a hang:

>>> hotp = pyotp.HOTP('base32secret3232')
>>> hotp.at(-1)

The at() method should probably raise an exception if it's passed a negative value.

If you try to filter for fields whose value is the empty string, it also matches records without those fields:

```
> http 'https://kinto.dev.mozaws.net/v1/buckets/46524be8-0ad7-3ac6-e260-71f8993feffa/collections/tasks/records?any_arbitrary_field=""' -a user:pass
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Expose-Headers: Next-Page, Pragma, Retry-After, Backoff, Alert, Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified, Expires, Total-Records, Content-Length
Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store
Connection: keep-alive

Content-Type: application/json
Date: Tue, 06 Jun 2017 20:50:47 GMT
ETag: "1496685126658"
Last-Modified: Mon, 05 Jun 2017 17:52:06 GMT
Server: nginx
Total-Records: 4
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff

{
    "data": [
        {
            "done": false,
            "id": "3dd095cb-f4f7-4c87-8112-6cd3e7660f41",
            "last_modified": 1496685088616,
            "title": "test"
        },
        {
            "done": false,
            "id": "0717608f-1cf2-472b-8a49-3136d2811946",
            "last_modified": 1496685088608,
            "title": "blbl"
        },
        {
            "done": false,
            "id": "65e5b714-dcb2-4136-836c-996e83514297",
            "last_modified": 1496524477816,
            "title": "to a poo"
        },
        {
            "done": false,
            "id": "5a1fc9a7-6c6a-428e-b932-930a69d6495a",
            "last_modified": 1496422987261,
            "title": "444"
        }
    ]
}
```

This is because of https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/core/storage/postgresql/__init__.py#L709. Postgres appears to be the reference implementation of Kinto's storage backend (see #815), so you might expect this behavior to leak into other backends too.

The [documentation](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/docs/api/1.x/filtering.rst) is pretty scanty on the subject of missing fields, saying only `Will return an error if a field is unknown`, and I think that's meant to only apply to `like_`.

I'm not 100% sure what we should expect this to do (much less how to implement it), but it seems like you'd expect missing fields to compare as different from everything, including null (see #1215), and not match `ILIKE`. In the tests, for instance, there's a couple tests that filter for `deleted != true` (which is intended to match "live" records) or `deleted == false` (which is intended not to match any records).

| Build System |  cmake | 

If callbacks are registered for each stream (color, depth, infrared, infrared2) with `set_frame_callback(...)`, but only infrared2 stream is enabled with `enable_stream(...)`, both the infrared2 AND infrared callbacks are called when a new frame is available.

See code example attached [cpp-callback-ir.cpp.txt](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/files/664808/cpp-callback-ir.cpp.txt)

It appears that calling `disable_stream()` unregisters the previously registered callback, but then maybe the registration method should be a parameter to `enable_stream(...)` instead of having a separate `set_frame_callback(...)` to be consistent. Or do not unregistered the callback in `disable_stream()`, but instead add a `clear_frame_callback()` to unregister to have symmetry.

issue_title: Potential security issues with `get_ip()` changes in v4
issue_body:
I'm a little concerned by the change to use `ipware.ip.get_ip()` in v4.

1. There is no documentation to suggest that `django-ipware` may need to be configured.
2. There is now no documentation on the potential attack vector if the proxy doesn't set `X-Forwarded-For` (or whatever the first header used in `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` is) explicitly, c.f. [`USE_X_FORWARDED_HOST`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/settings/#std:setting-USE_X_FORWARDED_HOST)
3. ~~`django-ipware` says to use `get_ip()` if the server is **not** accessible on the Internet, see [here](https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware#how-to-use).~~ (Although looking at the code the distinction between `get_ip()` and `get_real_ip()`, the latter doesn't count private IP addresses as "real". What they really mean here is use `get_ip()` if there are also clients that may connect from an internal private network otherwise use `get_real_ip()`.)
4. Previously `django-axes` allowed configuring the maximum number of proxies in case of spoofed entries. This is no longer possible. `django-ipware` provides `get_trusted_ip()` but it isn't particularly useful in it's current form.

It would probably be wise to recommend setting `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` to `['HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR', 'REMOTE_ADDR']` or at least indicate that this was the original behaviour. Checking all of these headers by default feels risky.

Forgive me, but this feels rushed and ill thought through. It was working fine for me and issue #275 and pull request #280 fail to even give any evidence of a problem. Every comment in #275 is regarding `AttributeError` when looking up `META` on `NoneType` - `django-ipware` is still using `request.META`. This feels like a push to remove code in favour of a dependency for minor gain.

issue_title: scipy_interfaces.fftpack needs _next_regular to support upstream changes
issue_body:
`_next_regular` is designed to support fft implementation specific optimisations. In the case of FFTW, this is different to fftpack, so a custom one should be included (it is used by scipy internal tools).

See https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/6124.

issue_title: Can't install new certificates with certbot-apache under Gentoo since version 0.20.0
issue_body:
It seems that version 0.20.0 has a major architecture revision from 0.19.0, in particular regarding OS specific overrides. But the Gentoo overrides have a flaw when trying to install a new certificate : at one point it tries to run "apache2ctl -t -D DUMP_MODULES", which is no good at all under Gentoo because apache2ctl is a dummy script redirecting to /etc/init.d/apache2. It generates this error :

`Error in checking parameter list: /etc/init.d/apache2: invalid option -- 't'`

## My operating system is (include version):
Gentoo

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
OS package manager (emerge) with testing (~amd64) flag

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
```
certbot -d test.example.com
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log
Plugins selected: Authenticator apache, Installer apache
Obtaining a new certificate
Performing the following challenges:
tls-sni-01 challenge for test.example.com
Waiting for verification...
Cleaning up challenges
Error in checking parameter list: /etc/init.d/apache2: invalid option -- 't'

Apache is unable to check whether or not the module is loaded because Apache is misconfigured.
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
Certbot did not install the certificate because it failed to run apache2ctl -t -D DUMP_MODULES

issue_title: errors dict in ValidationError for Flask-Restless support
issue_body:
sqlalchemy-validation is suggested as validation framework for Flask-Restless: http://flask-restless.readthedocs.io/en/stable/customizing.html#capturing-validation-errors

But the ValidationError does not contain the needed dict called "errors". Does anything speak against adding it to the constructor?

`class ValidationError(Exception):
    """ issued when models are flushed but have validation errors """
    def __init__(self, invalid_instances):
        self.invalid_instances = invalid_instances
        self.errors = dict()
        instance_errors = []
        for instance in invalid_instances:
            fields_with_errors = []
            fields = instance._sav.errors
            model = str(instance)
            for fname, errors in six.iteritems(fields):
                self.errors[fname] = errors
                fields_with_errors.append('[%s: "%s"]' % (fname, '"; "'.join(errors)))
            instance_errors.append('%s %s' % (model, '; '.join(fields_with_errors)))
        msg = 'validation error(s): %s' % '; '.join(instance_errors)
        Exception.__init__(self, msg)`

It is possible for a language to have multiple kernels, for example matlab has [`matlab_kernel`](https://github.com/Calysto/matlab_kernel) and [`imatlab`](https://github.com/imatlab/imatlab), and JavaScript has [`jupyter-nodejs`](https://github.com/notablemind/jupyter-nodejs) and [`ijavascript`](https://github.com/n-riesco/ijavascript). It is likely that a sos language extension could work with all of them so it makes sense to support multiple kernels in a single language extension.

Note that

```
%use iMatlab -k imatlab -l Matlab
```
could already be used to use `Matlab` language with `imatlab` kernel.

issue_title: Decouple client-server definition to allow more flexible configuration of servers.
issue_body: Right now we need to put a localized configuration file for all the machines that we would like to use. This can be inconvenient if we would like to change configuration for a server and error prone because we need to change all entries for all desktops. I would propose that we separate the definition of client and servers that connect them by sos.

It's a backwards incompatible change, but `--gh-pages-docs` defaulting to `docs` is not great. `.` is probably what people expect.  Maybe we should make it a required argument. 

CC @ocefpaf

Up to now, we compose different transforms via `Compose` transform, which works like `nn.Sequential` but for transforms. Each transform is applied independently to `input` and `target`.
While simple and efficient, there has been an increasing demand on extending the available transforms to accept both `input` and `target`, see #9, #115, #221 for some examples. This would allow performing the same random transformations in both the `input` and the `target`, for tasks such as semantic segmentation.

There are a few possible approaches and I'll summarize the ones that have been mentioned in here:
1. provide a set of random transforms working on pairs (or triplets, etc) of images, as proposed for example in #221. There are a couple of downsides of this approach: (a) it doesn't scale when we want to combine images coming from different domains (in object detection, we have image and bounding boxes for example, and we need to reimplement a new transform for each pair, introducing some redundancy in the code), (b) hard-coded to work with 2 inputs (but could be extended to `n`-inputs without difficulties)
2. factor out the randomness in the transforms, so that we can use the same functionality we currently have, just needing to add a `generate_seed` call in `__getitem__`, as was proposed in #115 . The drawback from this approach is that in some cases, we need the information of `input` to be able to perform the transformation in `target` (imagine flipping a bounding box horizontally, it requires the image width, which is not available right away).
3. provide a generic base class that samples the random transformation parameters, and passes those parameters to the input arguments, as proposed by @chsasank in [the slack channel](https://pytorch.slackarchive.io/vision/page-2/ts-1501788713664208). While this is an improvement over both #221 and #115, it still suffers from the same limitation of #115 as we still only pass one input argument at a time.

Also, all those options actually handle each input independently. Furthermore, for both 1. and 3., the order of the operations is fixed in the dataset class (first input transforms, then target transforms, then joint transforms, for example). There might be cases where it would be convenient to not be restricted to such orderings, and alternate between single transform and joint transform.

One possibility to address those issues would be to provide a set of functions like `nn.Split`, `nn.Concat`, `nn.Select`, etc, so that we can always pass all inputs to the transforms, and let the transforms be implemented by the user explicitly. This mimics the legacy `nn` behavior.
The downside of this approach is that it gets very complicated to write some transformations, and this doesn't buy us much.

Instead, one simpler approach (which has already been advocated in the past by @colesbury) is to let the user directly subclass the dataset and implement their complex transforms in there. This approach (4.) would look something like
```python
class VOCDatasetSegmentation(VOCDataset):
    def __init__(self, flip=False, **kwargs):
        super(VOCDatasetSegmentation, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        self.flip = flip

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        image, target = super(VOCDatasetSegmentation, self).__getitem__(idx)
        do_flip = np.random.random() > 0.5
        if self.flip and do_flip:
            # flip image and bbox here
        return image, target
```
A downside is that we can't easily re-use those transforms in a different Dataset class (COCO for example).

The question now is if there is an intermediate design that we could leverage that keeps the simplicity of 4., without having to subclass the dataset and reimplement every time the same transforms?

What about yet another possibility (5.) would be to let the user write the code of their as follows

```python
class Dataset(object):
    def __init__(self, transforms=None):
        self.transforms = transforms
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        # get image1, image2, bounding_box
        # the transforms takes all inputs into account
        if self.transforms:
            image1, image2, bounding_box = self.transforms(image1, image2, bounding_box)
        return image1, image2, bounding_box

from torchvision.transforms import random_horizontal_flip

class MyJointRandomFlipTransform(object):
    def __call__(self, image1, image2, bounding_box):
        # provide a functional interface for the current transforms
        # so that they can be easily reused, and have the parameters
        # of the transformation if needed
        image1, params = random_horizontal_flip(image1, return_params=True)
        # reuses the same transformations, if wanted
        image2 = random_horizontal_flip(image2, params=params)
        # no transformation in torchvision for bounding_box, have to do it
        # ourselves
        if params.flip:
            bounding_box[:, 1] = image1.size(2) - bounding_box[:, 1]
            bounding_box[:, 3] = image1.size(2) - bounding_box[:, 3]
        return image1, image2, bounding_box
```

In this way, we have the flexibility subclassing the dataset, while being more modular and easy to implement.
There would be some differences in the way we write our datasets currently, but we could have a fallback implementation for backward compatibility, in the lines of
```python
class StandardTransform(object):
    def __init__(self, transform, target_transform):
        self.transform = transform
        self.target_transform = target_transform

   def __call__(self, input, target):
        if self.transform:
            input = self.transform(input)
        if self.target_transform:
            target = self.target_transform(target)
        return input, target
```
and we would replace in the current datasets `transform` and `target_transform` by a single `transforms`, while keeping the old behavior

```python
class Dataset(object):
    def __init__(self, path, transforms=None, transform=None, target_transform=None):
        # assert that only transforms or (transform, target_transform) can be set at a time
        if transforms is None:
           transforms = StandardTransform(transform, target_transform)
        self.transforms = transforms

    # getitem only uses transforms from now on
```

What do you think? Do you see drawbacks on using such an approach?

issue_title: CIDR manipulation library functions would be useful
issue_body:
It would be useful to have an equivalent of the Terraform `cidrsubnet` function available as a library function for calculating subnet CIDR blocks from a base address.

From the Terraform documentation:

`cidrsubnet(iprange, newbits, netnum)` - Takes an IP address range in CIDR notation (like `10.0.0.0/8`) and extends its prefix to include an additional subnet number.

For example:
- `cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/8", 8, 2`) returns `10.2.0.0/16`
- `cidrsubnet("2607:f298:6051:516c::/64", 8, 2)` returns `2607:f298:6051:516c:200::/72`

issue_body:
Hi,

I am afraid to come with a form of specific request here. I opened this issue last week on Yandex/clickhouse repository: https://github.com/yandex/ClickHouse/issues/2605. It was about issues with support of specific column and types to store IPv4 and IPv6 data.

I didn't get any sort of positive answer from them... at least short term.

I was wondering if it could make sense to develop a form of additional types in your code such that column named IPv4_... or IPv6_... benefits from a specific behaviour. the idea would be to apply conversion function in you code and introduce those column as type INET (similar to postGRESQL) : https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.1/static/datatype-net-types.html.

By having this feature, This type could be handled by upper layers like your SQLAlchemy driver and related UI and Frontend.

Does it sounds like something doable / acceptable ?

Thanks Matthieu.

issue_title: Header guard handling conflicts with Google spec
issue_body:
According to the [Google guidelines](https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.    html#The__define_Guard), the format of the header guard is:
`{PROJECT}_{FILEPATH}_{FILENAME}_`
e.g. `HELLOWORLD_SRC_INCLUDE_HEADER_H_`

However, CppLint.py insists that the header guard format must be: `{FILEPATH}_{FILENAME}_`

We need a way to specify the $PROJECT

Also, the Google spec specifies that the header guard is an exception to the line-length limits, but cpplint.py does not treat it as an exception.

issue_title: ProjectFileManager.create doesn't cope with deep paths (file_path with slashes)
issue_body:
I've been trying to use gitlab.v4.objects.ProjectFileManager to create a file at a path within a repository, but gives a 404.

e.g. with `file_path="out/csv/Opportunity.csv"`

```
      response = p.files.create({
                'file_path': file_path,
                'branch': args.branch,
                'content': file_content,
                'encoding': 'base64',
                'commit_message': args.commit_message
                })
```

Gives

```
https://gitlab.int.corefiling.com:443 "POST /api/v4/projects/922/repository/files/out/csv/Opportunity%2Ecsv HTTP/1.1" 404 25
```

Note that the `.` has been encoded as `%2E`, but the occurrences of `/` have not been encoded as `%2F`, which seems to be what the CRUD API requires (from experimentation with `curl`).

I'm using python-gitlab 1.0.2, which doesn't do anything fancy with the file_path:

https://github.com/python-gitlab/python-gitlab/blob/1.0.2/gitlab/v4/objects.py#L1434

If I pre-process file_path (`file_path.replace("/", "%2F")`), the call succeeds.

Is the `/` encoding meant to be the responsibility of the caller, or is this something that `python-gitlab` should take care of?

GitLab 10.6 onward supports closed_by attribute for issues

https://docs.gitlab.com/ce/api/issues.html#list-merge-requests-that-will-close-issue-on-merge

Currently the only way to do this is iterating through merge requests and matching on the "closes" attribute.
totally unacceptable, @vsoch!

issue_title: ONNX doesn't have "pooling_convention" attribute  for pooling(Max/Avg)
issue_body:
For Pooling:
**pytorch** has " ceil_mode"  to specify which value(ceil/floor) to use while calculating output shape.
http://pytorch.org/docs/master/nn.html#maxpool2d

Similarly, **MXNET** has "pooling_convention" for using ceil or floor for  output shape from maxpool.
https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/symbol.html#mxnet.symbol.Pooling

But in **ONNX** pooling , there is  no attribute to determine if the input framework used ceil or floor for pooling operations? https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/6270bb68c54647f6c9ca56d640a73fe2dec2ad80/docs/Operators.md#MaxPool

Is there a reason ONNX does not have this attribute?  How can we determine this ?

@bddppq

issue_title: How to exchange code for credentials
issue_body:
Hi, 

the docs said it is [encouraged to post questions](http://oauthlib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/error_reporting.html) in the issues, so here I am.

When using (the now deprecated) oauth2client, in order to exchange authorization_code from credentials that I could later use with Google APIs I did this:
```
authorization_code = self.request.get('code')
flow = oauth2client.client.flow_from_clientsecrets(CLIENTSECRETS_LOCATION, ' '.join(SCOPES))
flow.redirect_uri = REDIRECT_URI
credentials = flow.step2_exchange(authorization_code)

# use Google APIs
GMAIL = discovery.build('gmail', 'v1', credentials=credentials)
```

I'm struggling to find compatible mechanism with OAuthLib, am I missing something?
Seeing that oauth2client sends users to use OAuthLib after the deprecation, an explanation on that matter would be very useful.
Thanks
